data.title: Lennix discusses Onchain OS and next-generation on-chain transaction models
data.description:
The annual Hong Kong Web3 Carnival has kicked off again, bringing together crypto professionals from around the world. As one of the largest and most influential Web3 events in the industry, the "2026 Hong Kong Web3 Carnival" features numerous keynote speakers and over a hundred exhibiting projects, attracting widespread attention and participation from developers, organizations, and industry stakeholders worldwide. On the first day of the summit, Lennix, Global Business Officer of OKX, was invited to attend and delivered a keynote speech entitled "Start building on Onchain OS" at the main forum. The following is the content of the speech (edited):

AI More Than Just a Tool Upgrade, It's the Beginning of a Trend

The past year to year and a half has seen very rapid changes. Everyone is talking about AI and using Agents. With the development of large-scale models, we're beginning to see Agents not just as a concept, but as something that's truly entering our businesses. We're seeing users creating all sorts of Agents to achieve different goals. Some people are using OKX Wallet data, combined with AI models, to monitor the market, generate signals, and then deploy different strategies.

These applications have made us realize that this isn't just a tool upgrade, but a significant emerging trend. And this trend could be very important because it will change trading behavior itself and the way we interact with on-chain transactions.

The changes over the past year to year and a half have been incredibly rapid. Everyone is talking about AI and using Agents. With the development of large-scale models, we're starting to see Agents not just as a concept, but as something that's truly entering our businesses. We're seeing users creating all sorts of Agents to achieve different goals.

In the past, OKX Wallet spent a lot of time optimizing the user experience, from Web2 to Web3, from the interface to the transaction process. However, if Agents become part of the user experience, or even the primary user, the entire interaction logic needs to be redesigned. The problem is clear: you can use Agents for deep data analysis and generate many trading signals using AI, but the most crucial step, or what Agents currently lack, is execution—and how to execute securely. How can we maintain user control without sacrificing Agent freedom? These are the truly difficult questions. We see that some Agents can already do some things, such as managing accounts, initiating payments, and performing simple operations, but these capabilities are fragmented. The problem isn't whether they have the capabilities, but that these capabilities haven't been integrated and run in a secure, controlled execution environment. So we started thinking: if Agents are users, shouldn't we build an operating system for them? This is our starting point. Onchain OS: An Operating System Born for Agents We don't just treat users as human users; we consider agents themselves as users. Therefore, we need a system that can serve different types of agents, enabling them to invoke capabilities, perform operations, and collaborate. Onchain OS isn't something built from scratch; it's constructed on top of the capabilities already available in OKX Wallet. OKX Wallet's system is API-first, processing over 1.2 billion API calls daily, handling approximately $300 million in transactions, and currently supporting over 60 chains and aggregating over 500 DEXs, with an overall system stability of 99.9%. Because everything is API-ready, we can build MCP on this foundation, allowing future agents to directly invoke these capabilities to accomplish what they want.

In this system, we've also introduced payment capabilities, allowing Agents to complete payments and transactions; using the X Layer as the execution layer results in extremely low costs, even free; and we've also introduced a Plugin mechanism, allowing the community and developers to build more capabilities. We hope users can directly perform actions using natural language, rather than clicking through layers of interfaces.

... For example, if you say, "When ETH drops to 2000 USDT, exchange it for USDC," the agent will continuously track market prices, use OKX Wallet data to make judgments, and then automatically complete price comparison, liquidity adjustment, verification, and execution of the transaction when the conditions are met. Therefore, future crypto users may no longer focus on the specific user interface as they do today, because all operations will be expressed in a common language. Underlying security, liquidity, asset management, and various complex processes will all be handled uniformly by the OKX Wallet native underlying system. But what's even more interesting is that this won't be a world with only one agent. In the future, there will be many agents. Your agent will interact with other agents, cooperate with third-party agents, and even generate payments and settlements between them. We will enter an environment of agent-to-agent and agent-to-human interaction. In such an environment, we need an infrastructure to support these payment scenarios, and this is the role of X Layer. It's a Layer 2 based on the Enhanced OP Stack, supporting fast, low-cost settlement, as well as payments and interactions between Agents. We believe that in the future, more and more Agents will participate in transactions and interactions on the blockchain, potentially forming an on-chain ecosystem far exceeding the scale of human-dominated systems. Therefore, to achieve this, a truly efficient settlement layer is needed. This is why we want to build an open ecosystem; this is just the beginning, and we need ecosystem partners to participate, such as continuously expanding different types of Skills. Simultaneously, we are continuously opening up the ecosystem, allowing the community to submit Skills, which are then reviewed by the AI ​​Agent and security team before being listed, and users can install them with one click. We also provide SDKs, CLIs, and other tools to help developers build Agents, making them closer to application forms. In Onchain OS and the Plugin Store, we hope to see more real-world use cases, ultimately enabling one person to manage projects or even the entire investment system.

Conclusion

For years, financial infrastructure has relied heavily on top professionals, requiring the formation of complete teams, including analysts, traders, risk controllers, and developers, as well as the establishment of a standardized investment process. It also needs to comply with anti-money laundering (AML) regulations and operate in tandem with such a system. But the future may be different. One person, along with multiple agents of different types, will have the opportunity to accomplish similar tasks, and even more efficiently.

When we interact with the on-chain ecosystem, we no longer need to rely entirely on teams to build all of this. Every user has the potential to gain capabilities and competitiveness approaching those of large institutions. This means that ordinary users can also possess advantages that were previously only available to professional institutions. I think this is a very crucial thing. This is not only a change happening in our ecosystem, but it also explains why AI is playing an increasingly important role in this industry.

We can even imagine that in the future, agent-driven transactions and interactions may exceed the scale of human-dominated markets.

Thank you everyone. If you are interested in Onchain OS and would like to work together, please feel free to contact us.

data.title: KelpDAO's one-click recovery of 30,000 ETH and Arbitrum's intervention shake the industry.
data.description:
Written by: jsai@Jinse Finance

On April 18, 2026, the DeFi sector experienced its largest attack so far in 2026.

KelpDAO's rsETH bridge (based on the LayerZero cross-chain protocol) was exploited by hackers to forge approximately 116,500 rsETH (worth approximately $292 million). The hackers minted unbacked rsETH by forging cross-chain messages, then quickly exchanged it for ETH and dispersed the funds on the Ethereum mainnet and Arbitrum One. Approximately 30,766 ETH (worth approximately $71 million) remained on the Arbitrum One chain.

On April 21, the Arbitrum Security Committee took rare emergency action, successfully freezing and transferring these funds.

Compared to the 2022 hack that stole 20 million OP tokens, in which Optimism explicitly refused to use an emergency upgrade to suspend or freeze token flow, this is the first known Stage 1 L2 (Arbitrum One, Optimism, Base, Starknet, etc.) to activate a security committee and freeze funds. This event demonstrates the crisis response capabilities of some L2 platforms, but it also quickly sparked a heated debate within the crypto community about the nature of "decentralization." I. Arbitrum Transfers Hacker Funds with One Click In a statement released on April 21, Arbitrum stated that after obtaining information from law enforcement regarding the attacker's identity, the security committee, after "extensive technical due diligence and review," implemented a "technical solution" to transfer 30,766 ETH from the hacker's address to an "intermediary frozen wallet."

Freeze transaction tx https://arbiscan.io/tx/0x5618044241dade84af6c41b7d84496dc9823700f98b79751e257608dac570f6b

This wallet can only be unlocked through further actions by Arbitrum governance and will not affect any other chain state, users, or applications.

This wallet can only be unlocked through further actions by Arbitrum governance and will not affect any other chain state, users, or applications.

The transfer was completed at 11:26 PM Eastern Time on April 20th, and the funds at the original hacker address are no longer accessible. This was a "surgical" intervention, not a full-chain suspension or hard fork. The Arbitrum Security Committee has taken immediate action to freeze 30,766 ETH held in the Arbitrum One address associated with the KelpDAO vulnerability. With the assistance of law enforcement, the Security Committee identified the attackers and has consistently prioritized the security and integrity of the Arbitrum community, ensuring that no Arbitrum users or applications are affected. After extensive technical investigation and deliberation, the Security Committee identified and implemented a technical solution to transfer funds to a secure location without affecting the state of any other chains or Arbitrum users. As of 11:26 PM ET on April 20th, the funds have been successfully transferred to an intermediate frozen wallet. The address that initially held these funds is no longer accessible, and further action to transfer these funds will only be possible after coordination between the Arbitrum administration and relevant parties. II. Detailed Explanation of the Freezing Mechanism: The Security Committee's Emergency Authority As an Optimistic Rollup on Ethereum (currently at Stage 1 of the L2Beat rating), Arbitrum's architecture incorporates a trade-off mechanism between decentralization and security. At its core lies the 12-member Security Committee (elected by the Arbitrum DAO), which possesses emergency escalation authority. The committee can upgrade or implement emergency measures through 9/12 multisignature authorization of time-sensitive system contracts, aiming to protect the DAO, users, and the entire ecosystem. This is not a "backdoor," but a publicly disclosed governance design to address hackers, vulnerabilities, or significant risks. This action was not a simple "address ban," but rather a targeted transfer of ETH held by a hacker, utilizing the Security Committee's upgrade capabilities. Arbitrum's Rollup mechanism allows for governance control of specific contract states or execution of special transactions in emergency situations without altering the overall chain consensus or affecting other addresses. According to on-chain analysis and technical reports, the core of this action was a temporary upgrade of the Inbox contract (the entry point for all Arbitrum→Ethereum messages on L1): 1. The Security Committee authorized an emergency upgrade via 9/12 multisignature: initiating a transaction on the Ethereum mainnet to upgrade the Inbox contract (or other related system contracts). After the upgrade, a new function is temporarily added, allowing cross-chain messages to be sent "on behalf of any wallet address"—without requiring the address's private key. 2. Forging a transfer message from a hacker's address: Using the new function, an L1→L2 message is constructed, impersonating the hacker's address as the sender, with the content "transfer all ETH from this address to the intermediate frozen wallet." This step essentially "signs" an L2 transfer on behalf of the hacker, but it is triggered by the security committee at the L1 layer. 3. Executing the transfer on L2: The message is executed on L2 via Arbitrum's Rollup mechanism, and the hacker's 30,766 ETH are directly transferred to the intermediate frozen wallet. Control of this wallet belongs solely to Arbitrum governance (it can only be unlocked through subsequent DAO voting). 4. Atomic Completion + Rollback Upgrade: The entire process (upgrade → forged message → execute transfer → remove new function/rollback upgrade) is atomically completed within a single Ethereum mainnet transaction. The upgrade is temporary, does not permanently change the contract logic, and does not affect the balance, contract state, or user interaction of any other addresses. In short: The hacker's ETH is still on the Arbitrum One chain, but the security committee "moved" the stolen ETH from the hacker's address to a frozen address controlled only by the DAO by forging a transfer message from the hacker's address. This demonstrates the practical trade-off between speed, security, and decentralization in L2. III. Discussion and Controversy in the Crypto Community This action quickly sparked polarized reactions on X (Twitter) and crypto forums. Many users praised this as a "correct and courageous decision": funds have been partially recovered (approximately 24% frozen), protecting users of protocols like KelpDAO and Aave and preventing greater systemic risks. Some joked that "decentralization is only needed when it's needed," pointing out that Bitcoin is the only chain that is "truly impossible to freeze," and that L2 is not purely decentralized. Some even argued that it would be dereliction of duty for a chain to fail to freeze illicit funds if it could. The security committee exists precisely for this purpose, acting quickly and transparently, more efficiently than some centralized stablecoin issuers (such as Circle). Arbitrum community members and representatives (such as Griff Green) even celebrated this as a "retaliation against hackers (suspected to be linked to certain countries)." At the same time, many voices of opposition and concern have emerged, which is the source of the controversy surrounding the Arbitrum Security Committee's recent actions. For example: Decentralization disillusionment: Many voices point to the fact that "this exposes Arbitrum as essentially a multi-signature wallet," and that the Security Committee's ability to unilaterally freeze funds in any address sets a dangerous precedent. "Today it's a hacker, tomorrow it could be an ordinary user?" "L2 decentralization is just a marketing term." Slippery slope concerns: Critics argue that while this move is "technically correct," it proves that L2 still relies on trusting a small group (the 12-person committee). In the future, if government pressure or governance is compromised, similar permissions could be abused. Some have declared, "I'll never use Arbitrum again, I'll switch back to L1." The Open Secret of Stage 1 Rollups: Supporters point out that this is a Stage 1 characteristic already marked by L2Beat (similar to most L2 systems like Base and Optimism), and not a sudden bug. However, opponents argue that users' misconception of "L2 = Decentralization" has been exposed by this incident, which has torn away the "last veil of pretense." Overall, the community consensus is that this is a necessary and effective crisis response in the short term, but in the long term, it highlights the need for L2 governance to evolve towards Stage 2 (fully decentralized without upgrade keys). This incident also brings the eternal DeFi debate of "freezing illicit funds vs. absolute uncensorability" back to the forefront. Conclusion: Realistic Choices for L2 Security The Arbitrum Security Committee's action successfully mitigated some of the losses, demonstrating L2's rapid response capabilities in the face of large-scale hacks. However, it also serves as a reminder to the entire industry: Currently, the vast majority of L2 systems remain "decentralized under governance protection," rather than operating under the "code is law" principle of L1. As DeFi expands, finding a balance between emergency intervention and minimizing long-term trust will be a crucial challenge for Arbitrum and the entire L2 ecosystem. For ordinary users, this may be a signal: when choosing a chain, consider not only TVL and fees, but also its governance transparency and emergency mechanism design. Decentralization in the crypto world is never absolute, but rather an evolving art of balance.

data.title: Saylor’s Strategy Makes One Of Its Largest Bitcoin Buys Since 2024, Adding $2.54 Billion In BTC Through Hybrid Funding Model
data.description:
Saylor’s Firm Adds $2.54 Billion In Bitcoin As Buying Spree Continues

Michael Saylor’s Strategy has expanded its Bitcoin holdings again with a $2.54 billion purchase, reinforcing its position as the largest corporate holder of the cryptocurrency.

The latest buy of 34,164 BTC was executed at an average price of $74,395 per coin, marking the firm’s biggest acquisition since late 2024.

The move pushes Strategy’s total holdings to 815,061 BTC, built up at an average entry price of $75,527, with the overall treasury now worth around $61.56 billion at current market levels.

SAYLOR STRIKES AGAIN: 800K BTC CLUB 🚀

MicroStrategy just added another 34,164 BTC for $2.5 Billion, bringing their total chest to a staggering 801,200+ Bitcoins.

At this rate, Michael Saylor is effectively sucking all available BTC liquidity off the exchanges. Is this the… pic.twitter.com/pzRoRfjxVP

— Coinlive (@CoinliveHQ) April 20, 2026

How Was The Latest Bitcoin Purchase Funded?

The bulk of the acquisition was financed through capital raised from Strategy’s STRC preferred shares, contributing about $2.18 billion.

The remainder came from common stock sales, which continue to add a degree of shareholder dilution.

STRC, a variable rate perpetual preferred stock, currently offers an 11.5% monthly dividend and has become a key funding tool for the company’s Bitcoin strategy.

Strategy has also reported a 9.5% year-to-date Bitcoin yield, highlighting gains from its aggressive accumulation approach under Saylor’s leadership.

Strategy has acquired 34,164 BTC for ~$2.54 billion at ~$74,395 per bitcoin and has achieved BTC Yield of 9.5% YTD 2026. As of 4/19/2026, we hodl 815,061 $BTC acquired for ~$61.56 billion at ~$75,527 per bitcoin. $MSTR$STRChttps://t.co/ifGXjMeIZH

— Michael Saylor (@saylor) April 20, 2026

Is Saylor Preparing For Another Bigger Buy?

Market attention has been drawn to Saylor’s recent social media activity, where he posted the firm’s familiar “Orange Dots” Bitcoin purchase chart alongside the message:

“Think even Bigger.”

The chart has historically preceded new buying announcements, particularly on Mondays, which has fuelled speculation that further acquisitions may follow soon.

Think Even ₿igger. pic.twitter.com/0Y8LgpPoFM

— Michael Saylor (@saylor) April 19, 2026

Earlier in April, Strategy added $1 billion in Bitcoin, following a $330 million purchase the week before, both also signalled in advance through similar posts.

Funding Strategy Shifts As Dilution Concerns Grow

Strategy’s Bitcoin accumulation has increasingly relied on hybrid funding tools, especially STRC, as it seeks to reduce reliance on common equity issuance.

While preferred shares avoid direct dilution, they carry a high dividend burden, raising ongoing financing costs for the company.

The shift comes after concerns grew around shareholder dilution during periods of weaker crypto prices, when the company previously leaned heavily on stock issuance to raise capital.

The STRC structure has also been adjusted recently, with plans to move from monthly to semi-monthly dividend payments.

The aim is to stabilise trading levels closer to par and improve flexibility in future capital raises.

Strategy is proposing to pay semi-monthly dividends on $STRC, instead of monthly. No change to the annual dividend obligations or dividend rate. These proposed changes are intended to stabilize price, dampen cyclicality, drive liquidity, and grow demand. pic.twitter.com/jHFRaDz6oP

— Michael Saylor (@saylor) April 17, 2026

Bitcoin Market Conditions Remain Stable Despite Activity

Bitcoin was trading around $76,504 at the time of reporting, showing modest gains of 1.92% over 24 hours and 2.29% over the past week.

The asset briefly crossed $78,000 over the weekend before easing back into its recent range between $65,000 and $75,000, a pattern that has persisted for much of the year.

Despite Saylor’s signalling and continued institutional accumulation, broader market momentum remains measured, with trading activity showing limited conviction.

Strategy Stock Moves With Bitcoin But Remains Under Pressure

Strategy’s shares were little changed at around $166, despite Bitcoin’s recent strength.

The stock remains down roughly 48% over the past year, even as Bitcoin has fallen around 11% over the same period.

Still, the company continues to lean into its Bitcoin-first treasury model, with Saylor repeatedly arguing that “Bitcoin has won” and that its long-term trajectory will be driven by capital flows rather than traditional market cycles.

data.title: a16z: Does the AI-driven agent economy lack infrastructure? Five key drivers from blockchain.
data.description:
AI agents are rapidly transforming from copilots into economic actors, far outpacing the development of the infrastructure surrounding them. While agents can now perform tasks and conduct transactions, they still lack standardized ways to prove who they are, what they are authorized to do, and how they are compensated across different environments. Identity cannot be transferred across environments, programmable payments are not supported by default, and collaboration occurs only in silos. Blockchain solves these problems at the infrastructure level. Public ledgers provide receipts for every transaction that can be audited by anyone. Wallets give agents portable identities. Stablecoins provide another settlement layer. These are not components from the future—they are available today and enable agents to operate permissionlessly as true economic actors.

1. Non-human Identity

（Identity for non-humans）

The bottleneck of the agent economy is now identity, not intelligence. In the financial services industry alone, the number of non-human identities—automated trading systems, risk engines, fraud models—is already approximately 100 times that of human employees. This proportion will continue to rise across industries as modern agent frameworks (tool-invoking large language models, autonomous workflows, multi-agent orchestration) are deployed on a large scale. However, these agents are essentially still in a “bankless” state. They can interact with the financial system, but not in a portable, verifiable, or default-trusted manner. They lack standardized ways to prove their authority, operate independently across platforms, or be accountable for their actions. What is missing is a universal identity layer—equivalent to an SSL certificate for the agent—capable of standardized coordination across platforms. While there have been some significant attempts, these approaches are fragmented: on one side are vertically integrated, fiat-first stacks; on the other are crypto-native, open standards (such as x402 and emerging proxy identity proposals); and there are extensions to developer frameworks, such as MCP (Model Context Protocol), attempting to bridge application-layer identity. Currently, there is still no widely adopted, interoperable way for one agent to prove to another who they represent, what they are allowed to do, and how they are compensated. This is the core idea behind KYA (Know Your Agent). Just as humans rely on credit history and KYC (Know Your Customer), agents will need cryptographically signed credentials to link them to their principals, permissions, constraints, and reputation. Blockchain provides a neutral coordination layer for this: portable identities, programmable wallets, and verifiable proofs that can be resolved across chat applications, APIs, and marketplaces. We've already seen early practices emerge: on-chain agent registration, native agents in wallets using USDC, ERC standards for "trust-minimized agents," and developer toolkits that combine identity with embedded payments and fraud control. However, until a universal identity standard emerges, merchants will continue to keep agents at bay.

2. Governing AI-run systems

（Governing AI-run systems）

The agents have begun operating real systems, which raises some new questions about "who is really in control". Imagine a community or company where an AI system coordinates critical resources—whether it's allocating capital or managing supply chains. Even if people vote on policy changes, this power is quite weak if the underlying AI layer is controlled by a single provider (who can push model updates, adjust constraints, or override decisions). The formal governance layer may be decentralized, but the operational layer remains centralized; whoever controls the model ultimately controls the outcome. When agents assume governance roles, they introduce a new layer of dependency. In theory, this could make direct democracy more feasible: everyone could have an AI representative to help understand complex proposals, model trade-offs, and vote based on stated preferences. But this vision is only possible if agents are truly accountable to the people they represent, are transferable across providers, and are technically constrained to follow human instructions. Otherwise, you get a system that appears democratic on the surface but is actually dominated by the behavior of an opaque model with no one truly controlling it. If the current reality is that agents are built from a few base models, then we need to prove that the agents are acting in the interests of their users, not the model companies. This will likely require providing cryptographic guarantees on multiple levels: (1) exactly which training data, fine-tuning, or reinforcement learning the model instances are derived from; (2) the exact cues and instructions the specific agent follows; (3) a record of its actual actions in the real world; and (4) a credible guarantee that once deployed, the provider cannot change its instructions or retrain it. Without these guarantees, agent governance degenerates into governance dominated by those who control the model weights. This is where cryptography is particularly useful. If collective decisions are recorded on-chain and executed automatically, AI systems can be required to adhere to verified results. If agents have cryptographic identities and transparent execution logs, people can check whether their agents are adhering to boundaries. If the AI ​​layer is user-owned and portable, rather than locked to a single platform, then no company can change the rules through model updates. Ultimately, governing AI systems is an infrastructure challenge, not a policy challenge. True authority depends on building strong, enforceable guarantees within the system itself. 3. Filling the Gap in Traditional Payment Systems for AI-Native Businesses Agents are starting to buy things—web scraping, browser sessions, image generation—and stablecoins are emerging as an alternative settlement layer for these transactions. Meanwhile, a new type of marketplace for agents is forming. For example, Stripe and Tempo's MPP marketplace aggregates over 60 services designed specifically for AI agents. In its first week, it processed over 34,000 transactions with fees as low as $0.003, and stablecoins are one of the default payment methods. The difference lies in how these services are accessed. There is no checkout page. The agent reads the schema, sends requests, pays, and receives information within a single transaction. They represent a new class of "headless" merchants: a single server, a set of endpoints, and a price per call. There is no front end—neither a storefront nor a sales team. The payment methods that make all this possible are already online. Coinbase's x402 and MPP take different approaches, but both embed payments directly into HTTP requests. Visa is also expanding card payments in a similar direction, providing a CLI tool that allows developers to make payments from the terminal, with merchants receiving stablecoins immediately on the backend. The data here is still early. After filtering out unnatural activity (such as wash trading), x402 processes approximately $1.6 million in agent-driven payments per month, far less than the $24 million recently reported by Bloomberg (citing data from x402.org). But the surrounding infrastructure is expanding rapidly: Stripe, Cloudflare, Vercel, and Google have all integrated x402 into their platforms. Developer tools represent a significant opportunity, and as "vibe coding" expands the pool of who can develop software, the market for solving new types of developer problems is growing. Companies like Merit Systems are building AgentCash for this world—a CLI wallet and marketplace that connects to both MPP and x402. These products allow agents to purchase the data, tools, and capabilities they need using stablecoins in a single balance. Thus, agents on a sales team can enrich leads with data from Apollo, Google Maps, and Whitepages by calling a single endpoint, without the user leaving the command line. This agent-to-agent (A2A) business activity tends to shift towards crypto solutions (and emerging card solutions) for several reasons: one is underwriting, where a payment provider assumes the risk of a merchant when the merchant is onboarded. A headless merchant without a website or legal entity is difficult for traditional processors to underwrite. Secondly, stablecoins are permissionless and programmable on open networks: any developer can enable a terminal to make payments without integrating a payment processor or signing a merchant agreement. We've seen this model before. Every business model shift creates a new type of merchant that existing systems initially struggle to serve. The companies building this infrastructure aren't betting $1.6 million per month; they're betting on what that number will look like when agents become the default buyers. 4. Repricing Trust in an Agentic Economy (Repricing trust in an agentic economy) For 300,000 years, cognition has been a constraint on human progress. Today, AI is pushing the marginal cost of execution to zero. When scarce resources become abundant, constraints shift. When intelligence becomes cheap, what becomes expensive? Validation. In the agent economy, the real limit to scalability is our biologically limited ability to audit and assume machine decision-making responsibilities. The throughput of the agent economy has far exceeded human oversight capabilities. Because oversight is expensive and faults are delivered with delays, markets tend to deliberately ignore it. "Timely synchronization with humans" is rapidly becoming physically impossible. But deploying unvalidated agents introduces compound risks. The system ruthlessly optimizes the intentions of "agents," subtly deviating from human intent and creating an empty facade of productivity that masks the debt incurred by the large-scale adoption of AI. For our economy to be securely entrusted to machines, trust can no longer rely on human checks—trust must be hard-coded into the architecture itself. When anyone can generate content for free, verifiable provenance becomes paramount—knowing where it comes from and whether you can trust it. Blockchain, on-chain proofs, and decentralized digital identity systems are changing the boundaries of a securely deployable economy. You no longer treat AI as a black box, but gain a clear, auditable history. As more AI agents begin to transact with each other, settlement and verification become complementary. Systems of fund flows—such as stablecoins and smart contracts—can also carry cryptographic receipts showing who did what and who is responsible if things go wrong. Human comparative advantage shifts upward: catching small mistakes, setting strategic direction, and taking responsibility when things go wrong. Sustainable advantage belongs to those who can cryptographically verify outputs, endorse them, and take responsibility for failures. Unverified growth at scale inevitably becomes a liability that accumulates over time.

5. Preserving user control

（Preserving user control）

data.title: The KelpDAO hack has impacted the entire DeFi industry. How can the massive bad debts be resolved?
data.description:
On April 18th, KelpDAO's rsETH cross-chain asset suffered a massive attack, resulting in a loss of approximately $290 million (about 116,500 rsETH, representing 18% of the circulating supply). LayerZero Labs, in its latest statement, initially attributed the attack to the North Korean Lazarus Group (also known as TraderTraitor). The attackers poisoned the downstream RPC nodes of LayerZero DVN and, in conjunction with a DDoS attack, induced a failover, causing DVN to confirm that "the transaction did not occur," thus forging cross-chain messages. While this incident did not directly target the Aave protocol itself, it triggered a systemic liquidity shock unprecedented in DeFi history. Aave's TVL plummeted by approximately $8.45 billion within 48 hours (latest monitoring indicates a decrease of approximately $8-9.5 billion), shrinking the total TVL of DeFi across the entire chain from $99.497 billion to $86.286 billion, a loss of $13.21 billion. The event quickly spread from the EVM chain to Solana, Kamino, and other lending protocols, with USDC utilization rates instantly surging to 100%, putting the DeFi industry into a "stress test" mode. LayerZero Official Disclosure: Single Point of Failure, Not a Protocol Vulnerability LayerZero Labs released a detailed statement on April 20th, confirming that the attack precisely targeted KelpDAO's 1/1 single DVN configuration (relying solely on LayerZero Labs' validator), deviating from its consistently recommended multi-DVN multi-redundancy best practice. The attacker used poisoning, binary substitution, and DDoS-induced failover to make DVN confirm "no transaction occurred," but did not exploit any protocol or key vulnerabilities. The impact was limited to rsETH and did not spread to other OApp/OFT assets. LayerZero has scrapped and replaced all affected RPCs, DVN is back in operation, and all 1/1 configuration projects are required to migrate to multi-DVN as soon as possible. Simultaneously, they cooperated with global law enforcement agencies to track funds. However, Yearn Finance core developer banteg explicitly questioned this description. He stated that characterizing the incident as "RPC poisoning" is inaccurate and confuses the concepts. In traditional cyberattacks, RPC poisoning involves attackers tampering with shared lookups (DNS, ARP, cache, etc.) outside the trust boundary, leaving the recipient with no reason to suspect the source. But in this attack, the attackers penetrated LayerZero's trust boundary, accessing the RPC list, compromising two nodes relied upon by DVN, and replacing the op-geth binary. This is closer to an infrastructure breach or supply chain attack than typical external network poisoning. banteg believes this description underestimates the severity of the attack and advises against rushing to restore the cross-chain bridge until the specific source of the vulnerability is clearly identified. Aave Takes the Helm: Approximately $200 Million in Bad Debts + Liquidity Run Following the attack, hackers used stolen rsETH as collateral to borrow ETH on Aave, resulting in approximately $195-216 million in bad debts that could not be recovered through regular liquidation. Aave V3's ETH utilization rate in multiple markets reached 100% at one point, triggering a massive withdrawal wave—Justin Sun alone withdrew approximately 65,500 ETH. DeFiLlama data shows that Aave's TVL shrank by over $8 billion in the past two days, and market panic spread. Spark's Head of Strategy, monetsupply.eth, pointed out that Spark delisted low-usage assets such as rsETH and tightened the scope of collateral as early as January. Although this resulted in a short-term loss of some business, current results prove this prudent strategy effective: the SparkLend ETH market maintains ample withdrawal liquidity, while the Aave multi-chain market has experienced a liquidity shortage. How to handle the massive bad debts? Three Paths Await

DeFiLlama co-founder 0xngmi's analysis indicates that KelpDAO faces three main processing paths:

1. Loss Shared Among All Users:This corresponds to approximately 18.5% write-down, resulting in approximately $216 million in bad debt. Of this, Umbrella ETH can cover approximately $55 million, the Aave Treasury will provide an additional coverage of approximately $85 million, and the remaining shortfall of approximately $76 million can be filled by borrowing or selling approximately $51 million worth of AAVE tokens from the Treasury.

2. Losses concentrated among L2 rsETH holders: This could result in approximately $341 million in bad debts, uncovered by Umbrella. 3. Compensation based on a pre-attack snapshot: Due to significant fund flows and the pooled structure of the protocol, execution is difficult. Even so, approximately $91 million in losses may remain after Umbrella coverage. OneKey founder Yishi stated that the optimal solution is to "negotiate with the hackers and offer a 10-15% bounty"; if negotiations fail, the LayerZero ecosystem fund can bear the majority of the losses. KelpDAO, as the "weakest party," can make up the difference through tokens or future revenue, or consider selling itself entirely to LayerZero (L0) or BMNR. Aave's Umbrella and stkAAVE form the last line of defense, but he also warned that WETH depositors should not accept write-downs, otherwise protocols such as Morpho, Spark, Fluid, and Euler may face repricing risks, damaging overall trust in the LRT sector. Currently, Aave's Umbrella security module is facing its first real stress test, and whether it can fully cover the gaps remains uncertain. Ethereum Foundation's Positions in Focus, Industry Confidence Shaken DeFi analyst Ignas points out that the Ethereum Foundation (EF) holds approximately $48 million in assets on the Aave Ethereum mainnet. If there is no write-down in the mainnet market, it will be more beneficial to the safety of EF's positions. EF had previously avoided DeFi for a long time, only recently starting to allocate to "low-risk DeFi," and this event may affect its subsequent decisions. More broadly, the event has triggered industry reflection on: the trust risks of external LST/LRT collateral, the potential for single-point-of-failure in cross-chain bridges, and the liquidity mechanisms of DeFi under extreme pressure. Solana's Kamino Prime Market USDC Reserve utilization reached 100%, with multiple Vaults exceeding 95%, indicating that capital flight has spread across chains. Is this DeFi's "coming of age" or a turning point? This isn't a code vulnerability in Aave, but rather a systemic chain reaction triggered by "misplaced trust in collateralized assets." As many analysts have stated, KelpDAO's 1/1 DVN configuration and the excessive reliance on external collateral between protocols are a microcosm of collective risk. LayerZero emphasizes that "modular security architecture has isolated the impact," but the industry still needs stricter collateral access mechanisms, mandatory multi-DVN standards, and a more robust bad debt sharing mechanism. Whether Aave can weather this storm with Umbrella will be a crucial indicator of DeFi resilience. In the short term, TVL outflows and panic may continue; in the medium to long term, if bad debts can be digested in an orderly manner and drive protocol upgrades, this event may become a significant milestone in risk management. Otherwise, as Yishi worries, rebuilding trust in the LRT sector and even the entire DeFi ecosystem may take much longer.
